--- title: 'calibrateR' permalink: /tools/calibrateR --- 2021-05-22-calibrateR.utf8

Description

calibrateR is a package written to streamline common laboratory calculations. At the moment, this includes SEC calibration, BCA calibration, protein and nucleic acid concentration determination using UV/Vis spectroscopy, and the estimation of protein parameters given just a primary amino acid sequence.

Example files

More in-depth example usage of calibrateR that goes through more of the function options is provided on GitHub.

Installation

devtools::install_github("gbedwell/calibrateR")

SEC calibration

There are two primary outputs for the sec() function:

  1. A calibration output that calculates a standard curve given the necessary calibrant information.

  2. An analytical output that estimates the molecular weight and hydrodynamic radius of a given analyte.

Both outputs can be generated in either tabular or graphical forms.

Generating a calibration curve

At least 3 different pieces of information are required for calibrateR to generate a calibration curve for a size-exclusion column:

  1. The position of the column void volume. This is defined as void in the sec function. This can be empirically defined by running the polymer blue dextran over the column.

  2. The column volume. This is defined as cv in the sec function. This can be empirically defined by running something like 0.2% (v/v) acetone over the column.

  3. The masses (masses) and elution volumes (stds) of a series of standard proteins. If using the commonly used BioRad gel filtration standards, the masses flag can be left NULL.

Toggle between tabular and graphical outputs with the plot flag. Setting plot = “yes” will generate graphical output. plot = “no” generates tabular output. Tabular output includes linear regression information.

To generate the calibration curve, set type = “cal”.

The hydrodynamic radii of the standard proteins are estimated based on their masses using the relationship described by Uversky (Biochemistry. 1993. 32. 13288-13298).

sec(masses = c(669000, 158000, 44000, 17000, 1350),
    void = 1.87,
    cv = 4.29,
    stds = c(2.08,2.58,2.98,3.30,4.06),
    unk = NULL,
    plot = "no",
    type = "cal")
## Using defined mass values instead of default values!
mw.da rh.nm standards.ev ve.v0 K.av mw.pred mw.lwr mw.upr rh.pred rh.lwr rh.upr mw.r.sq rh.r.sq
669000 7.86 2.08 0.21 0.09 723568.81 593856.67 881613.10 8.09 7.52 8.71 1 1
158000 4.62 2.58 0.71 0.29 150680.84 131366.18 172835.32 4.54 4.31 4.77 1 1
44000 2.88 2.98 1.11 0.46 42945.79 38233.66 48238.67 2.85 2.73 2.98 1 1
17000 2.03 3.30 1.43 0.59 15732.97 13852.11 17869.21 1.97 1.88 2.07 1 1
1350 0.80 4.06 2.19 0.90 1448.94 1165.48 1801.33 0.82 0.75 0.89 1 1

Estimating analyte properties

Setting type = “pred” will predict the molecular weights and hydrodynamic radii of unknown samples. This is based on the calculated standard curve and analyte elution volumes.

Define the analyte elution volumes as unk in the function.

In the graphical output, analyte parameters are reported in labels on the plot itself.

sec(masses = c(669000, 158000, 44000, 17000, 1350),
    void = 1.87,
    cv = 4.29,
    stds = c(2.08,2.58,2.98,3.30,4.06),
    unk = c(2.11, 2.49, 3.28, 3.87),
    plot = "yes",
    type = "pred")
## Using defined mass values instead of default values!
##
## Molecular weight and radius LABELS appearing on the plot are NOT log-transformed!
##
## NOTE THAT THE ACTUAL POINTS ARE STILL AT LOG-TRANSFORMED POSITIONS!

BCA calibration

The function for BCA calibration (bca) is generally similar the sec function.

The function minimally requires:

  1. The known concentrations and corresponding absorbance values for a standard dilution series (prot.conc and std.abs).

  2. The absorbance value of the buffer-only control (blank.abs).

bca(prot.conc = c(2, 1.5, 1, 0.75, 0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.025),
    blank.abs = 0.1813,
    std.abs = c(2.4644, 1.989, 1.4295, 1.1075, 0.8124, 0.499, 0.3655, 0.2315),
    abs = NULL,
    dil.fac = NULL,
    plot = "no",
    type = "cal")
conc abs corr.abs conc.pred conc.lwr conc.upr r.sq
2.00 2.46 2.28 1.95 1.88 2.01 1
1.50 1.99 1.81 1.53 1.49 1.58 1
1.00 1.43 1.25 1.04 1.01 1.08 1
0.75 1.11 0.93 0.76 0.73 0.79 1
0.50 0.81 0.63 0.51 0.48 0.54 1
0.25 0.50 0.32 0.23 0.20 0.27 1
0.12 0.37 0.18 0.12 0.08 0.16 1
0.02 0.23 0.05 0.00 -0.04 0.05 1

To estimate the protein concentration in unknown samples, the absorbance values and dilution factor the sample(s) must also be defined (abs and dil.fac).

The plot and type flags operated identically to the sec function.

bca(prot.conc = c(2, 1.5, 1, 0.75, 0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.025),
    blank.abs = 0.1813,
    std.abs = c(2.4644, 1.989, 1.4295, 1.1075, 0.8124, 0.499, 0.3655, 0.2315),
    abs = c(2.0184, 1.1641, 0.5831),
    dil.fac = c(5, 10, 20),
    plot = "yes",
    type = "pred")
## The concentrations reported as labels on the plot are the DF CORRECTED values!
##
## These represent the final values that should usually be used in downstream calculations!
##
## For multiple dilutions of the same sample, the mean of these values should be calculated.

Protein parameter estimation

Protein parameter estimation from the primary amino acid sequence is a useful tool for any protein chemist. There are numerous webtools that estimate various parameters in this way. The prot.param() function in calibrateR calculates many standard parameters as well as some less common parameters that are useful for hydrodynamic analyses.

To estimate these parameters, first define the primary amino acid sequence of the protein-of-interest.

Next, define the temperature at which the experiments are being performed. This is important for the estimation of the protein’s partial specific volume (vbar) and associated parameters (e.g. the radius of a sphere with equal mass and density).

protein <- c("MSKGEELFTGVVPILVELDGDVNGHKFSVRGEGEGDATNGKLTLKFICTTGKLPVPWPTL
VTTLTYGVQCFSRYPDHMKRHDFFKSAMPEGYVQERTISFKDDGTYKTRAEVKFEGDTLV
NRIELKGIDFKEDGNILGHKLEYNFNSHNVYITADKQKNGIKANFKIRHNVEDGSVQLAD
HYQQNTPIGDGPVLLPDNHYLSTQSVLSKDPNEKRDHMVLLEFVTAAGITHGMDELYK")

calibrateR::prot.param(protein, temp=25)
parameter value
number of residues 238.000
isotopically averaged mass (Da) 26780.874
monoisotopic mass (Da) 26764.436
vbar at temp (mL/g) 0.735
folded molar ext. coeff. at 280 nm (OD/mol*cm) in H2O 19160.000
unfolded molar ext. coeff. at 280 nm (OD/mol*cm) in 6 M GuHCl 17450.000
molar ext. coeff. at 205 nm (OD/mol*cm) in water 909360.000
radius of sphere with equal mass and density (nm) 1.984
est. denatured radius (nm) 5.001
est. globular radius (nm) 2.322

```
